sentences.text: Stary Wisnicz.
I was just boarding the-- the-- the train with my father.
It was the first day, it was Friday, the September 1st.
Well, we were en route, but where, we didn't know.
Just out of there.
No, no.
Right when they started to give us trouble, we-- we found a place in Wisnicz Nowy.
We moved there.
This was 1940-- end of 1940.
Something of the sort.
Yeah.
We went to-- we rent a sort of place they used to have-- an oil depot room.
So we-- we made this our-- our home, sort of.
My mother decorated and put the separation for us and for the children.
And, you know, one of those things was ours.
You know, it was cold in winter, very cold, because it was a cement floor.
You know, and it was not comfortable.
But we were family.
We were together, that's what counts.
Yeah.
There was no school for Jewish children.
But there were some separate things that we were teaching her.
And some other people were teaching her.
The school, per se, was no school.
Well we were working, as I told you.
We were cutting wood.
There we were renting ourselves out.
Not my sister or my mother, no.
And then, in the new place in Wisnicz Nowy, what happened there?
How did you-- how did you live there?
We lived there until 1942.
In the meantime, I met Sally, so-- Sally is your wife?
Sally is my wife.
We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- I wouldn't use the words part of a community, but we stood there, and we tried to make the best of it.
But we never lost hope that the things would come to an end and we'd persevere.
And that's what kept not only us-- I think everybody had the same-- not opinion, but hope-- that this would have to come to an end, sooner or later.
And we did not know that it was only the beginning of the end.
Because at that time, compared to others, it wasn't that very bad-- comparing to ghettos.
Because this was still open areas.
Always the same rules.
Always, it was tight.
Eventually, it became a rule in 1942, that all Jews must evacuate Wisnicz Nowy, and surroundings, and they would be all sent to labor camps.
Yes, I met Sally.
So I told you probably that whatever this-- I worked with-- with some girls from there, and--
In-- in-- in the castle.
It was a castle in Wisnicz Nowy-- it was a castle from the time of King Sobieski in Poland who was instrumental in stopping Genghis Khan in Vienna.
Someplace there.
So it must have been 400, 500 years old.
And the Germans had a headquarters there.
There was a POW camp of Germans over there.
So they had X amount of Jews to do manual labor.
So I was one of the manual laborers, and the girls were also part of it.
That's how we got together.
They said they had some friends to meet, and we met separately.
We tried to make as much social life as possible, so we shouldn't be stagnant, so to speak.
So that's how everything started.
There were no social activities.
We were not allowed to any meetings at all.
Everything was on penalty of death.
No meeting, no nothing.
But we got together anyway.
You know, my brother got together with his age group.
You know, we young-- one year makes a lot of difference.
So he got together with his age group, I got together with my age group of people.
In one of the houses.
Because people had their own houses.
And we had-- some-- some nights we went-- as a matter of fact, sometimes we were dancing also, if you call it this way.
Someone's harmonica, or something.
We shouldn't-- we shouldn't be all black.
We should always have hope.
Only hearsay.
Because you couldn't have a radio.
No way-- now way could you have a radio.
Death.
Everything is death.
You're here to a foreign station.
That was your death.
There was no-- there was no other laws.
Everything was penalty of death.
But if you read the German papers, you could read between lines.
If you get a hold of a paper.
Some, they-- like in Stalingrad, they were saying that they-- they're withdrawing according to the plan.
So according to the plan, you knew they were in trouble.
Every time they were in trouble, there were more Jews being killed.
Anywhere.
Anywhere.
Yes.
They'll take it out any way.
No.
No.
No.
Not at that time.
But they-- when they-- when we heard that the area had to be liquidated, that-- according to this, and people sent to camps, that time we went to-- we decided not to go.
Not to listen to this.
And we made an arrangement-- arrangement with a Polish farmer.
In June '42.
Yeah.
The summer of '42.
The order was given that all the Jews from surrounding areas had to clear the area, period.
They said in the labor camps.
Different area.
Yes.
Yeah, depot.
Right.
Right.
My family says, no way.
And I went to Sally's family, and said, listen, there's no such thing at the depot.
So they went-- Sally want that I should come with them, and I wanted Sally to come with us.
But we decided to go with our families.
To a farmer.
And we stayed there for about a week or so.
Four of us.
Because my brother worked for the Germans.
They were building truck frames-- frames for trucks.
So he was sort of-- he had a pass, so he was free of all this.
And he says, go ahead.
He was the one who told us, go ahead, hide.
When it will be all clear, I'll come after you.
No, he was-- he was staying there, on-- on the-- on the-- on the premises.
No, not where we had lived.
He worked in the Bochnia ghetto.
So he was travelling there, to Bochnia ghetto.
So he was-- because he happened to have a pass, because he was a mechanic and he was mechanically inclined.
So he was working there.
And so when this happened, we went to Bochnia ghetto.
No, I don't remember.
I don't remember.
My father.
My father connected with him.
Yes.
Yes.
Whatever we could, we paid him.
Some clothing, something, you know.
He didn't guide me, we were staying by him.
Yes, yes, sure.
In the-- in the cellar, all this time.
No.
Not that-- not that far.
Only in the houses, where they used to live.
That's where the searches were made, but not outside the area.
So I mentioned my brother gave us a message we should come.
And also, you had to smuggle yourself in.
You just couldn't go this way.
How did you do that?
At night-- during the night, we went.
Then he told us more or less which way to go.
We went.
It wasn't so strict at that time like later on.
And we stayed there for a while, in ghetto, itself.
In the ghetto.
This area designated for Jews.
We had a room.
We-- my-- because a lot of people were evacuated from the area.
Then they made the ghetto.
So there was a room at that time.
We came with my aunt, also.
We gave her what used to be, in the beginning-- we had to come together.
Aunt Malka.
She was my father's younger sister.
We were very close together with her.
Very close.
Bochnia ghetto-- [SIGHS] everybody had a room, only the family had the room.
And some of them lived there before.
They were not refugees, or they came from there.
They were moved from one place to another.
And they were divided into areas-- one, a working area.
One, a non-working area.
And the Germans saw to it that not everybody is working.
So they had to-- ones who are they make-- they made-- they killed people, period.
They killed-- the area with-- people who did not work, could not produce passes that they're working.
They killed a lot of people.
Machine guns.
In the open street.
Go to houses, kill them.
No, we were hidden that time.
We were [NON-ENGLISH], we were [NON-ENGLISH].
My brother told us one time, he says, look-- it was in March in '43-- he says, look, they're supposed to liquidate the area B ghetto.
So we made bunkers for ourselves.
He covered everything.
Someone had to be outside covering everything.
And we were hidden.
After we heard the shots, silence, everything, we came out.
You were in this area B?
Yes.
No, you couldn't get a pass.
They gave passes mostly to people who come from there.
You know, you had to know somebody-- who is who.
So we were refugees.
You know-- one of those things.
Everybody says the same thing.
Some houses were deteriorated from this, so we bought the remnants of the house.
We cut up the wood.
And we sold the wood to people for the wintertime.
You know-- we persevered.
For bread, we had enough for bread.
In the meantime, I worked outside.
I started-- I got-- No, I was caught at the time, and sent to a labor camp in Plaszow.
Labor, not concentration camp.
It was a labor camp.
It was 19-- beginning of '43.
Let's say you're walking in the street.
They catch you.
It's the law.
We need so many people.
Taken off the street.
Yeah, middle of the week.
Yeah.
They took me to Plaszow.
I was there for a while.
Yeah, I stayed in Plaszow.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I-- I gave them a message.
I sent them-- it wasn't that far from Bochnia to Krakow.
It wasn't that far.
So whoever was there, I gave them my message.
In the message I said, I'm all right.
You know--young guy, 18 years old.
You know-- if you don't have high hopes, you're just-- you're down to earth, and you more or less absorb it, so to speak, quote unquote.
And I spoke German also, so I acquainted with all the German soldiers who were issuing passes.
So he gave me a pass and he told me I should issue passes.
And he signed it.
Passes that you could go home and come back.
There were passes for certain [INAUDIBLE].
So every Friday night, really, I tried to go home to Bochnia.
And they didn't touch me, because I had a pass that I am working.
So they didn't touch me.
I was working there by earth moving.
Simmons Bauer is still now-- the Germans still have it.
It's a very big company.
And we were laying tracks.
And I was part of a group like this.
And once in a while, I was going-- going sort of home to Bochnia ghetto.
And it was there.
And this happened to me two or three times.
By the fourth time-- they were catching me-- by the fourth time, they caught my father.
Because my father was in-- in the 40s, at that time.
So I went ahead and I said, I'll be smart.
I said look, I exchange for my father.
OK?
What do you care?
You need so many people.
What do you mean that they caught?
Off the street.
They caught you off the street in the-- in the ghetto B, the people who are not working to-- to camp.
And I saw the same thing happened again.
So I went and I exchanged for my father.
I said, Dad.
He says, you go ahead.
I said, what are they going to do?
I have a pass.
I don't want to show the pass here, but I will show the pass over there that I don't belong to them.
They caught me.
But that was the third time-- but at the time, they caught me and they sent me to a concentration camp, in-- in Plaszow.
This was 1943 in-- it was in the end of summer.
Yeah, working part of Plaszow.
This is a concentration camp.
There were two different areas.
In the barracks.
Germans.
But there was Jewish, and then Germans.
But there was no hitting or anything in the labor camps.
There was no hitting.
Here, you know, the German masters, they were hitting-- if they wanted to.
They had full power of life and death, if they wanted to.
But I spoke German, so I didn't-- more or less, you had to play by ear.
Most of them, yeah, at that time.
At that time.
There was never adequate food.
But you managed.
They gave you soup and a piece of bread.
It-- it was-- was sort of not enough to live, and too much to die-- type of thing-- in the labor camp.
In the concentration camp, it was different.
Some.
Some.
But nobody close, really, I could consider friends.
Yeah.
More or less, yes.
My brother was working, as I told you, for the Germans.
And my father and my uncle, my aunt's husband, they were both doing these things with the wood.
And they were managing in the ghetto.
But there always a constant fear of-- of killing, because they were like an open season over there, in the ghetto B.
No, not disappearing.

They would kill people on the outside of the ghetto that were caught without passes, without-- they say, oh, they caught some Jewish girls who somebody pointed at them, that they were Jewish-- Jewesses.
They killed them.
No.
I heard about it.
When I was sent to Plaszow, things were entirely different.
First of all, you were surrounded by barbed wire, all around you.
You couldn't get out of there.
You-- you were part of a barrack.
And the barrack was called Bochnia Barrack, because all those who was caught there-- there was from Bochnia ghetto.
And-- And you were under strict orders, working area, where you got up 6 o'clock in the morning and you worked until dusk.
And-- and the kapo-- there was a Jewish kapo-- who-- or a Jewish supervisor-- who beat you quite a while.
It did not happen to me, but I saw it happening.
And a German master was over all you, and he was-- he was a master of life and death, also.
And on top of it, the commander of the camp was the famous Goeth.
And he was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how do you describe him?
Human hell.
Because no matter where you worked, you worked.
And he was always going to the gun, and-- and-- and the German shepherd with you.
If you-- if you looked at him, he didn't let you look.
He just shot you, that's all.
He would let the dog run over you, without provocation, without anything, without ifs or buts.
In the meantime, from our barracks, somebody ran away.
And they took 10 people from the barracks at random, in the middle of the camp, and they shot them-- over the four who were running away.
The second time someone ran away, again, and they shot 20.
At random.
So I-- in the meantime, I was with a friend of mine, also from Wisnicz.
His name was Abush Reich, was his name.
I said, Abush, the law of averages are against us.
I said, we have to get out of here.
We were walking one Sunday afternoon-- we had the free time only Sunday afternoon.
We were walking and we saw a big line over there.
So we asked-- in the camp itself.
In the meantime, I heard the whole Bochnia ghetto is liquidated.
And there's nobody left there-- in ghetto B.
In the total ghetto, the total ghetto B is being liquidated.
So nobody told me something about what happened to my family.
Nothing.
Prior to that, I forgot to tell you, my brother was arrested by the Jewish police in Bochnia ghetto, and he was accused of belonging to the Jewish underground.
And that's the last I heard of him.
I had in my mind to tell you this, but something happened.
And that's the last I heard of him.
Yes, he was.
And I didn't know, because he didn't want to tell me.
Because if he tell me, and they catch me, I would sort of spill the beans.
I just found out from somebody-- somebody else.
So I knew that he was a [NON-ENGLISH], like an interrogation center in Krakow, and then he was sent to Auschwitz.
So in the meantime, I knew that when they liquidated the ghetto that nobody was surviving.
So I said to Abush, I said, Abush, we have to get out of here.
This doesn't-- doesn't work.
So there was a big line over there.
I said, what's this line for?
They said, you know what?
This line is for a guy named Schindler-- Oskar Schindler.
I said, who is he?
He said, he needs some people to work for him outside the ghetto.
I said, Abush, I don't care where he is.
As long as we get out of here, we stay in line.
We stayed in line, standing with about 400 people.
How many people he needs?
He said, about 100.
I said, Abush, we're never going to make it.
In the meantime, the Jewish police, the camp Jewish police are minding order in line.
And they're hitting here, hitting there.
One guy called somebody.
He said, Steiner, just take care of this over here.
I heard the word, Steiner.
And my mother's name older is Moehler, at that time, many names they have.
And she came from a Steiner family.
So I-- I go up the line, and the guy wanted to hit me.
I said, wait, Steiner.
I said, hold it, hold-- I said, I think that we are related.
We are related?
You and I?
I says, maybe.
I said, do you come from Bielsko?
He said, yeah, what's your business?
I says, I think that my-- you and my mother are cousins.
He said, where do you come-- what's your mother's name?
I said, Sara, [NON-ENGLISH].
He said, [NON-ENGLISH], where do you come from?
I said, Chorzow.
Are you Sarah's son?
I says, yeah.
What are you doing here?
What do you mean, what am I doing here?
Look at me, I'm not waiting for a bus.
Of course, I'm doing here-- I want to go to-- out of here.
I said, I'm from Bochnia Barracks.
He says, OK.
So he said, come with me.
I said, look, I have a friend of mine and we're going together.
He grabbed us both, put in front of the line.
This how I end up with Schindler.
So you never can tell, you see, how things turn out.
That's how I ended up by Schindler, eventually.
When I was by Schindler, I was working--
I was taken to Schindler.
Right then and there.
We were assigned to--
To outside.
Schindler has his barracks outside the camp, in Amalia.
Separate barracks.
We were staying there.
We were staying there, we' had a sign over there.
[SIGHS] Must have been the end of summer '43, or beginning of fall '43.
I don't remember the exact date.
I knew only it was a good day at that time.
We were assigned to a special group of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] construction.
We had the foreman, and we had thin boys like me working construction outside.
Not inside.
Inside was nice and warm.
But I said, I don't care.
Whatever it is, it's better than over there.
That's how we wound up by Schindler.
And we worked there.
And anything there, compared to Plaszow, was-- was heaven, really.
Yes.
What happened is this.
At one time, Oskar Schindler always was coming.
He said, how are you?
How are you doing?
You know, plain words.
So we told him.
So one Sunday afternoon, he gets the guy, the chief of the Jewish police, he said, let them play a little bit of ball in the afternoon.
They gave us a soccer ball and we played in the afternoon.
So I-- I was good player, so I scored a couple of goals that time.
So the chief of police says, why-- where-- who are you?
Where do you work?
He came to me, says, oh, I see you're a good player.
Where do you work?
I said, in construction.
He said, why do you work construction?
He said, come on, I'll give you a job in inside.
And I said, look, I have a policy. sort of a policy-- we work together.
I don't push, I don't press.
I want to work with him together.
He said, any time you want to come inside, tell me.
Two weeks later, the whole police, the whole higher echelon was send to Mauthausen.
Everyone except the construction, except us.
All Jewish, all Jewish.
Part of Schindler's group.
They all were sent to Mauthausen.
All.
This group remained, and a few more.
From inside the camp--
Schindler's camp.
I don't know.
Goeth must have, or somebody else must have told him we have too many workers here-- complained, for the production that you're issuing, you have to liquidate.
So that's what happened.
No, once they went to Mauthausen, they were gone.
They were gone there.
Most of them, a lot of them persevered.
But you know, we stayed there.
And-- and eventually, something happened.
I'll tell you later.
OK.
We'll pause here for a tape change.
